@maximbaz Weird. Is it possible that it's the same behavior on the old module too? You mentioned last week or so that you don't use this anymore. Also, I ran this diff.

```
diff --git a/py3status/modules/static_string.py b/py3status/modules/static_string.py
index dbcec8c6..7d0158d0 100644
--- a/py3status/modules/static_string.py
+++ b/py3status/modules/static_string.py
@@ -16,12 +16,26 @@ class Py3status:
     """
     """
     # available configuration parameters
-    format = 'Hello, world!'
+    format = '{result}'
 
     def static_string(self):
+        cmd = 'xrandr -q | grep " connected [^(]" | cut -d " " -f1'
+        original = self.py3.command_output(cmd, shell=True).splitlines()
+
+        outputs = self.py3.command_output(['xrandr']).splitlines()
+        new = [x.split()[0] for x in outputs if ' connected' in x]
+
+        result = original == new
+
+        self.py3.log('-' * 15)
+        self.py3.log(original)
+        self.py3.log(new)
+        self.py3.log('-' * 15)
+        self.py3.log(result)
+
         return {
             'cached_until': self.py3.CACHE_FOREVER,
-            'full_text': self.py3.safe_format(self.format),
+            'full_text': self.py3.safe_format(self.format, {'result': result}),
         }
 
```

Yes, definitely replace charvector with emptyCharArray.

I prefer leaving the .value in there.  if we pass the lenout variable to stypes.emptyCharArray(), it is changed (back) to an int as xLen internally; that change is not passed back to the lenout variable outsiide the stypes.charvector scope, but I write in so many languages, some of which _would_ change the variable in the outer scop, that I'd rather not have to think about whether such things might happen.  Even Python can change a keyword argument in some cases e.g. lenout.values += 1; okay, that that is not the argument itself, but using .value spares me having to think about that kind of thing, which is the point.

But if you prefer, I can remove the functionally superfluous .values.  Let me know.

Sphinx 1.4 doesn't seem to support `extern` yet:

```
WARNING: Invalid definition: Expected identifier in nested name, got keyword: extern [error at 6]
  extern int foo
  ------^
```
Doxygen version 1.8.6 doesn't put `extern` in the `definition` field, but IIRC some other version do. Therefore I suggest keeping this.

This way of handling an empty speech parameter isn't very ideal (especially for responses which represent the interaction between the program and the user).

If no speech is passed, the user will hear "Error: Please add text...", but the developer will not notice this problem unless they study the logging statements.

Instead of passing the error message to the response as speech, try raising an appropriate exception or logging the message as a warning or error so that the developer can quickly see the issue.

Dockerbinary specifies whether the command has to be executed in nvidia-docker (bringing GPU support) or in regular docker (to run on CPU-only instances). We can't modify docker_run because it's a shared template and every task has to specify the docker-binary it has to run in. In the current CI, the docker-binary is determined automatically, depending on the name of the Dockerfile - but I'd like to follow a declarative approach instead of automatic assumptions and hardcoded names.

@AllisonWang  Here it is much faster. The join is super fast because there are good indices for it. The old logic is pretty slow because it makes a separate query for each task instance, and it is done synchronously. The amount of time taken by the join is likely <100ms, whereas the old approach is (database roundtrip + query_time) * (number of TIs). In practice, probably a minute in some of the worst cases.

Are we absolutely sure there isn't a problem with mutating the request as it's in flight through the requests/httplib code?  Seems like this is very sensitive to race conditions.  Also we read through the request body as we sign the code.  I assume this could cause issues if we're also trying to send the request at the same time.

This also means that the number of times this request is proportional to the concurrency level which I'm not really a big fan of.  I assume for things like sigv4 payload checksums this can get expensive.

This is something we have to consider, but here some benchmarks: 
```python
# from Django config, just to simulate a small set of configurations with an empty dict
DEFAULTS = {'AGENT_HOSTNAME': 'localhost',
 'AGENT_PORT': 8126,
 'AUTO_INSTRUMENT': True,
 'DEFAULT_DATABASE_PREFIX': '',
 'DEFAULT_SERVICE': 'django',
 'DISTRIBUTED_TRACING': False,
 'ENABLED': True,
 'INSTRUMENT_CACHE': True,
 'INSTRUMENT_DATABASE': True,
 'INSTRUMENT_TEMPLATE': True,
 'TAGS': {},
 'TRACER': 'ddtrace.tracer'
}
# test 1
DEFAULTS.copy()
# test 2
copy.deepcopy(DEFAULTS)
```
And here the results:
```
test 1: 0.00654911994934082
test 2: 0.2787208557128906
```
The huge overhead is because of the string copy, so if nested dictionaries are used and we go with a `deepcopy`, we should take in consideration such overhead.

Deque operations are thread safe as long as you treat it is a single sided queue and only pop from the opposite side that is being pushed to. Queue is built on top of deque and adds in optional features such as thread-safety for fixed size queues, as well as blocking puts and pops if the queue is empty/full respectively. With the current implementation we don't need the specific implementation of either so we can probably standardize to one  for ease of development. Queue has slightly worse performance but has more features so that would likely be the best choice.

As long as it is obvious to a user/caller how to get a higher value (and easy in a CLI implementation) then I am ok with having an internal default, I just want to make sure it is well documented so users understand what the intention is. As it was a user would think that adding more threads wouldn't give value or would not work, potentially.

Ok, now I know what you mean. I understand the idea, but there are some issues that I see:

1. when "top up" is used, people think about `added_amount` - how much they are going to pay and not about `total_balance` - how much they will have in the end. When you go to top up / recharge your phone card, you don't tell&give the cashier  the `total_balance`, but the `added_amount`.

2. This amount of tokens from the constructor has to also be approved if using an ERC20 token. It's weird to have an approve transaction for `added_amount` and then another channel-related one with `total_balance`.

3. If you use something like ERC223, you have to provide the `added_amount`.

Bottom line, I think it's better to use `added_amount` and make sure the UI is nice and clear for the user and I think it makes more sense when you compare with everyday payments.

The problem is that on purpose `pytrip` is not on the list of requirements for pymchelper.
Support of pytrip related functions is for "advanced users" who will install pytrip by themselves.
It was done like this for two reasons:
 - most of pymchelper users work with simple Fluka/SH12A simulation and don't need such heavy dependency
 - installation of pytrip under Windows and old Linuxes is problematic - it depends on many packages (scipy/paramiko etc)

I'd reccommend to do it in following way:
 - make an pytrip import and if it fails, then write a message to the user: "please install pytrip with `pip install pytrip98`"
 - if it succeeds then set the version to correct one
 - if it fails, then stop further execution
